Margi Murphy So we’re heading to an apartment block in downtown LA to go and see the apartment that’s registered as the address for Sol Ecom, Inc., which is the owner of drawnnudes.io.

Olivia Carville We were hearing of more cases like the Levittown one, tied to a handful of deepfake apps. We wanted to know who was behind them.

(Sound of knocking, and door opening)

Margi Murphy Hi, my name’s Margi. Are you—

Olivia Carville That’s next time on the final episode of Levittown.

Margi Murphy This series is reported and hosted by Olivia Carville and me, Margi Murphy.

Produced by Kaleidoscope — led by Julia Nutter, edited by Neda Toloui-Semnani, producing by Dara Look-Potts. Executive produced by Kate Osborn. Original composition and mixing by Steve Bone.

Patrick Carey’s statement is read by actor Jackson Ark.

Our Bloomberg editors are Caitlin Kenney and Jeff Grocott. Additional reporting by Samantha Stewart. Sage Bauman is Bloomberg's executive producer and head of podcasting. Kristin Powers is our senior executive editor.

From iHeart, our executive producers are Tyler Klang and Nikki Ettore. Levittown is a production of Bloomberg, Kaleidoscope and iHeartPodcasts. If you liked this show, give us a follow and tell your friends.

item.title: Labour Party Tax Raid on Private Schools Is Starting to Unravel
item.description:
No fewer than four eminent King’s Counsels have been hired to defend the government’s tax raid on private education in the High Court next week. The stakes are high. Levying so-called value-added tax on independent schools was one Labour’s few stand-out commitments in an otherwise threadbare election manifesto. The future of a ministerial star, Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson, also hangs in the balance.

All four of the appointed lawyers were educated at Britain’s finest independent schools — but they won’t be using that in evidence. This year, their alma maters have raised their fees by between 12.6% and 21.3% to offset VAT, while loudly protesting against the imposition of a tax on education, a rarity anywhere in the Western world. The government had insisted that private school fees would only rise by 10% in response to the change.

It doesn’t take a master satirist to see the irony in an egalitarian government relying on the privately educated elite to do its dirty work. The case reveals a national neurosis about Britain’s ancient fee-paying schools. Some of the best establishments in the world are in the UK — but there’s resentment against those who enjoy the benefits of a privileged education.

VAT on school fees at first looked like a win-win for Phillipson and Labour. The plan was to use the proceeds of the 20% levy to pay for 6,500 extra teachers in England’s state schools, raised from notional revenues of £1.6 billion ($2 billion) a year. There were more than a few votes to be had in robbing rich Peter to pay for the education of poor young Paul — only 7% of children attend private schools in the UK and the British are still absurdly chippy about class. So far, so popular.

The ambitious Phillipson was initially delighted to be making all the right enemies — the Tory press, the rich and foreign residents. Labour members, made increasingly glum by economic austerity, cheered the radicalism of the education secretary. She gained further plaudits from the left and soft left by proposing to restrict the freedoms of independent state academy schools at the behest of the teaching unions. The activist website Labour List puts Phillipson near the top of its league table for popularity.

Next week’s judicial review, however, is not a straightforward contest between toffs defending their privileges and the state acting in defense of “the many, not the few,” as Labour leaders are wont to say. Claimant groups include schools that cater for children with special educational needs (SEN) and those with a Christian ethos. Richly endowed schools such as Eton College — which has educated a succession of prime ministers and plutocrats — aren’t directly represented except by an industry body. Eton and its ilk can already write off some of the tax against capital expenditure on the gleaming facilities that dazzle prospective parent customers.

Embarrassingly for a government led by a former human rights lawyer, claimants are citing the Human Rights Act to advance their case. Lawyers for the Treasury - Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves is the official respondent to the lawsuit as it involves a tax measure — will argue that the court must uphold a measure passed by Parliament and put to the voters at the election in Labour’s manifesto.

Represented by David Pannick, one of the most learned human rights lawyers in the land, the claimants argue they’re not challenging Parliament’s rights. They seek a “declaration of incompatibility,” arguing that the government has passed a law flouting centuries of existing legislation, including education acts, charity laws and the recent Human Rights Act. The government also acted arbitrarily in the middle of the school year to deprive children of their access to education, it’s alleged, while the tax discriminates against those with special education needs and a parent’s right to uphold their religious beliefs.

Earlier this year, the House of Commons Public Accounts Committee found that the state’s provision for SEN and children with disabilities was mired in red tape, lacked funding and was “failing to produce value for money.” In summary, the system was judged to be “in disarray.” More than 10,000 children have been removed from private schools following the imposition of VAT, many in the middle of the academic year.

There does seem to be something very odd about the UK being one of the few countries in the world to single out education for a tax — not on university fees, nor nursery education, but one restricted solely to fee-paying schools. Parents who send their kids private have already paid their taxes for other peoples’ children to be educated.

Whatever the judges rule, Labour comes out of this story with little credit. At the previous election, the party’s policy-lite manifesto was a catalogue of evasions. The party didn’t use its long years in opposition to prepare for office and resorted to cheap class envy to fill out its prospectus.

The other populist tax in the manifesto — on wealthy foreigners — has already failed the reality test. The government has lost revenue by driving many so-called non-doms abroad to Milan, Dubai and other welcoming jurisdictions. Too little, too late, the Treasury has amended its tax regime. The UK’s seventh-richest man, the steel tycoon Lakshmi Mittal, a resident for 35 years, has just announced he’s leaving too.

Labour marketed itself as the party of “wealth creation” during the election, and promised not to increase the main revenue raising taxes — on income, VAT and corporate profits. Both Labour and the Conservatives, however, failed to come clean that taxes would have to rise unless public services were cut. After its election victory, Labour duly hammered business by increasing payroll taxes and so brought economic growth shuddering to a halt. A populist tax on education was just the shiny wrapping on an empty parcel — and the lawsuit may lay bare the hollowness of even that policy.

More from Bloomberg Opinion:

Want more from Bloomberg Opinion? OPIN . Web readers, click here . Or subscribe to our daily newsletter .
